examples.title: Hash Table Insertion using Linear Probing
examples.description: Implements insertion in a hash table with linear probing for collision resolution
examples.code:
PROCEDURE Insert(hashTable, tableSize, key)
BEGIN
  hashIndex := key MOD tableSize
  originalIndex := hashIndex
  
  WHILE hashTable[hashIndex] is not EMPTY and hashTable[hashIndex] != key DO
    hashIndex := (hashIndex + 1) MOD tableSize
    
    IF hashIndex == originalIndex THEN
      PRINT "Hash table is full"
      RETURN
    END IF
  END WHILE
  
  hashTable[hashIndex] := key
  PRINT "Key inserted at index ", hashIndex
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm computes initial hash index using division method. When collision occurs, it checks consecutive positions (linear probing) until an empty slot or the key itself is found. Uses modulo to wrap around table end.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) for table of m buckets

examples.title: Hash Table with Chaining Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of hash table using chaining for collision resolution
examples.explanation: Each bucket in the table is a Python list (acting as a chain). When collision occurs, new elements are appended to the list at that bucket. Search requires traversing the list at computed index.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) where n is keys and m is buckets

examples.title: Double Hashing Implementation in C
examples.description: C program implementing hash table with double hashing for collision resolution
examples.explanation: Two hash functions are used: h1(k) = k mod 11 and h2(k) = 7 - (k mod 7). The probe sequence is (h1 + i*h2) mod m, which distributes keys more uniformly and avoids clustering problems of linear probing.

examples.title: Rehashing Implementation in Java
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating automatic rehashing when load factor exceeds threshold
examples.explanation: When the ratio of elements to table size (load factor) exceeds 0.75, a new larger table is created (typically double size), and all existing elements are re-inserted with recomputed hash addresses. This maintains O(1) performance.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized for insert with occasional O(n) rehash
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) for hash table storage, temporary O(n) during rehash
